name: Increasing ideality
description: Systems evolve toward increasing ideality — maximizing useful functions while minimizing harmful functions and costs. The ideal system performs its function without existing.

stages.name: Initial system
stages.description: The system exists as a separate, tangible device with significant costs and harmful side effects.

stages.name: Improved system
stages.description: The system is optimized to reduce costs and harmful effects while maintaining useful functions.

stages.name: Function transferred to supersystem
stages.description: The system's function is absorbed by a neighboring system, eliminating the original system.

stages.name: Ideal system
stages.description: The function is performed without any system — by using available resources or by eliminating the need for the function.

name: Stages of evolution (S-curve)
description: Every technical system passes through four stages of evolution: infancy, growth, maturity, and decline, following an S-shaped curve of performance vs. time.

stages.name: Infancy
stages.description: The system is invented. Performance is low, many problems, few adopters. High inventive effort to overcome fundamental contradictions.

stages.name: Rapid growth
stages.description: Key contradictions resolved. Performance improves rapidly. Many patents, growing market. System differentiation begins.

stages.name: Maturity
stages.description: Performance approaches physical limits. Improvements become incremental. Focus shifts to cost reduction and feature additions.

stages.name: Decline / Transition
stages.description: The system cannot improve further within its current operating principle. A new system based on a different principle begins to replace it.

name: Transition to supersystem
description: Individual systems combine with other systems to form supersystems with enhanced capabilities and emergent properties.

stages.name: Mono-system
stages.description: A single, standalone system performing one primary function.

stages.name: Bi-system
stages.description: Two similar or complementary systems combined to gain new properties (stereo sound from two speakers, binoculars from two telescopes).

stages.name: Poly-system
stages.description: Multiple systems combined (Swiss Army knife, smartphone combining phone + camera + computer + GPS).

stages.name: Integrated supersystem
stages.description: Elements merge and boundaries dissolve. The supersystem becomes a new mono-system at a higher level.

name: Transition to micro-level
description: Systems evolve from macro-scale mechanisms to micro-scale and field-based mechanisms for finer control and higher efficiency.

stages.name: Macro-level mechanism
stages.description: The system uses macro-scale mechanical parts (gears, levers, springs).

stages.name: Molecular/chemical level
stages.description: Functions performed by chemical reactions, molecular interactions, or material properties.

stages.name: Field-based mechanism
stages.description: Functions performed by fields (electromagnetic, acoustic, thermal) instead of substances.

stages.name: Quantum/atomic level
stages.description: Functions performed at quantum or atomic level, exploiting quantum effects.

name: Increasing dynamism and controllability
description: Systems evolve from rigid and static to flexible, dynamic, and increasingly controllable, ultimately becoming self-adaptive.

stages.name: Rigid system
stages.description: The system has a fixed structure and cannot adapt to changing conditions.

stages.name: Jointed system
stages.description: The system has joints, hinges, or articulations allowing limited movement and adjustment.

stages.name: Flexible system
stages.description: The system uses flexible or elastic materials and can continuously adapt its shape.

stages.name: Field-controlled system
stages.description: The system uses fields (pneumatic, hydraulic, electromagnetic) for shape change and control.

stages.name: Self-adaptive system
stages.description: The system senses conditions and automatically adapts its structure, behavior, or properties via feedback.

name: Uneven development of parts
description: Different components of a system evolve at different rates, creating internal contradictions that drive further evolution.

stages.name: All components at similar level
stages.description: System components are balanced in their level of development.

stages.name: Key component advances
stages.description: One component evolves faster than others, creating bottlenecks in the lagging parts.

stages.name: Bottleneck creates contradiction
stages.description: The mismatch between advanced and lagging components creates a system-level contradiction that limits performance.

stages.name: Lagging component upgraded or replaced
stages.description: The bottleneck is resolved by upgrading the lagging component, often using a fundamentally different approach.

name: Increasing coordination (matching/mismatching)
description: Systems evolve toward better coordination between their elements and with the environment — matching rhythms, frequencies, and interfaces.

stages.name: Uncoordinated elements
stages.description: System elements operate independently without harmonization.

stages.name: Partially matched elements
stages.description: Some coordination introduced — resonant frequencies matched, interfaces aligned, rhythms synchronized.

stages.name: Fully coordinated system
stages.description: All elements work in harmony. Frequencies, geometries, and timing are optimized as a whole.

stages.name: Intentional mismatching
stages.description: Deliberate mismatching introduced for beneficial effects (e.g., spread-spectrum signals, anti-resonance damping).

name: Increasing automation (human involvement reduction)
description: Systems evolve to progressively reduce human involvement in performing the main function, from fully manual to fully autonomous.

stages.name: Manual system
stages.description: Humans perform the function directly using hand tools.

stages.name: Mechanized system
stages.description: Machines perform the physical work; humans control and monitor.

stages.name: Automated system
stages.description: The system operates automatically with human oversight and decision-making.

stages.name: Self-controlling system
stages.description: The system makes decisions autonomously using sensors and algorithms.

stages.name: Fully autonomous system
stages.description: The system operates, maintains, and improves itself without human involvement.
